CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized and the main contributions (Tandem Anchors, pseudoword construction, and interactive speedups) are stated early and carried through to the experiments. Notation appears consistent and the methodological steps (augmenting S, DKL reconstructions, Bayes’ rule recovery) are presented clearly, with effective figures/tables and examples that illustrate multiword anchors versus single-word anchors. A few presentation gaps remain: the paper omits hyperparameter details and code availability (which hinders reproducibility even as a presentation issue), and the discussion of synthetic pseudowords could use clearer justification and more explicit linkage to the experimental settings (e.g., metadata reliance). Overall the writing is good with minor clarity issues in those areas. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and presents the main contributions (tandem/multiword anchors, pseudoword construction, fast interactive use) clearly and early, with a logical flow from method to experiments using 20NEWS and reasonable baselines. Notation and method description appear consistent and well-defined, and writing quality is reported as good; experiments and stated limitations are clearly communicated. Minor presentation gaps remain for an interactive-practitioner audience (e.g., no mention of released code or reported hyperparameters), which prevent a top score despite overall clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized and the main contributions (Tandem Anchors, pseudoword construction, and interactive speedups) are stated early and carried through to the experiments. Notation appears consistent and the methodological steps (augmenting S, DKL reconstructions, Bayes’ rule recovery) are presented clearly, with effective figures/tables and examples that illustrate multiword anchors versus single-word anchors. A few presentation gaps remain: the paper omits hyperparameter details and code availability (which hinders reproducibility even as a presentation issue), and the discussion of synthetic pseudowords could use clearer justification and more explicit linkage to the experimental settings (e.g., metadata reliance). Overall the writing is good with minor clarity issues in those areas.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized and presents the main contributions (tandem/multiword anchors, pseudoword construction, fast interactive use) clearly and early, with a logical flow from method to experiments using 20NEWS and reasonable baselines. Notation and method description appear consistent and well-defined, and writing quality is reported as good; experiments and stated limitations are clearly communicated. Minor presentation gaps remain for an interactive-practitioner audience (e.g., no mention of released code or reported hyperparameters), which prevent a top score despite overall clarity.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is squarely within the NLP/ML interactive topic-modeling area and targets the right audience by proposing a concrete algorithmic extension (multiword/tandem anchors) and comparing to relevant baselines (single-word anchors, Fast ITM, Utopian). However, from a theory-focused perspective the work omits formal analysis of when pseudoword constructions succeed, provides only a single dataset (20NEWS), and does not report code or hyperparameters, which weakens reproducibility. Given these concerns but acknowledging the clear methodological contribution and relevant empirical comparisons, I judge it a good fit for a top venue though not a perfect one. | B(4): This paper sits squarely in the interactive topic-modeling niche important to top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a practical algorithmic extension (multiword/tandem anchors), emphasizes interactivity and scalability, and compares against relevant baselines (single-word anchors, ITM, Utopian) using standard clustering and coherence metrics on 20NEWS. From a human-in-the-loop perspective the paper’s focus on intuitive multiword anchors, pseudoword construction for guided editing, and claimed speedups are strongly relevant. Minor concerns for a top venue are the reliance on a single dataset (and metadata for some experiments), and missing details such as released code/hyperparameter reporting, which would strengthen reproducibility and broader applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is squarely within the NLP/ML interactive topic-modeling area and targets the right audience by proposing a concrete algorithmic extension (multiword/tandem anchors) and comparing to relevant baselines (single-word anchors, Fast ITM, Utopian). However, from a theory-focused perspective the work omits formal analysis of when pseudoword constructions succeed, provides only a single dataset (20NEWS), and does not report code or hyperparameters, which weakens reproducibility. Given these concerns but acknowledging the clear methodological contribution and relevant empirical comparisons, I judge it a good fit for a top venue though not a perfect one.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely in the interactive topic-modeling niche important to top NLP/ML venues: it proposes a practical algorithmic extension (multiword/tandem anchors), emphasizes interactivity and scalability, and compares against relevant baselines (single-word anchors, ITM, Utopian) using standard clustering and coherence metrics on 20NEWS. From a human-in-the-loop perspective the paper’s focus on intuitive multiword anchors, pseudoword construction for guided editing, and claimed speedups are strongly relevant. Minor concerns for a top venue are the reliance on a single dataset (and metadata for some experiments), and missing details such as released code/hyperparameter reporting, which would strengthen reproducibility and broader applicability.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper compares against sensible interactive baselines and uses standard metrics on 20 Newsgroups, and that reported runtime improvements are relevant for interactivity. They also agree on notable gaps: limited evaluation to a single corpus, missing hyperparameter/code disclosure, and lack of comparison to stronger statistical baselines or formal analysis of the pseudoword heuristic. Given the practical strength of the comparisons but these important omissions, a score of 4 reflects a solid but not fully comprehensive empirical evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against reasonable interactive baselines (single-word anchors, Interactive Topic Model / Utopian) and evaluates on standard metrics, but only reports results on 20 Newsgroups and does not mention code or hyperparameter detail, raising reproducibility and fairness concerns. It acknowledges limitations and contrasts its algorithmic differences, yet omits comparisons to strong statistical baselines (e.g., standard LDA Gibbs/variational estimators or recent likelihood-based samplers) and lacks formal analysis of when the pseudoword heuristic succeeds. Overall the comparisons are useful but incomplete for a theory-focused assessment.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against sensible and practical baselines including the original single-word anchor method and interactive, sampling-based approaches (Interactive Topic Model / Fast ITM and Utopian), and evaluates on standard 20NEWS with multiple clustering and coherence metrics. Comparisons appear fair in that common metrics and the same dataset are used, and the authors report runtime advantages relevant to interactivity. However, minor gaps remain: hyperparameter/reporting details and code availability are not mentioned, and evaluation is limited to a single corpus which weakens generality—hence not a fully comprehensive comparison but still strong and pragmatic.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, practical bottleneck in interactive topic modeling and introduces a usable, efficient multiword-anchor approach that is likely to influence tooling and workflows. However, limitations in reproducibility (no released code or hyperparameter details), restricted empirical validation (single dataset), and lack of formal guarantees temper its transformative potential; these concerns reduce but do not negate its likely real-world impact, so a score of 4 reflects strong practical contribution with room for broader validation and theoretical/reproducibility improvements.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important practical problem—making interactive topic modeling faster and more user-friendly—and presents a scalable multiword-anchor approach that appears useful for interactive settings compared to sampling-based ITM baselines. However, from the summary I see no released code, no hyperparameter details, and no formal guarantees about when pseudoword construction or multiword anchors succeed, which limits reproducibility and theoretical credibility for the community. The reliance on metadata for high-water-mark experiments and synthetic pseudowords also constrains broader applicability. Overall this is a useful, pragmatic contribution likely to be adopted in interactive NLP work but not yet transformative for the field without stronger empirical/theoretical backing and reproducibility artifacts.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a clear, practical bottleneck in interactive topic modeling—making anchors more intuitive and fast to use—by introducing multiword "tandem anchors" and a simple pseudoword construction that supports human guidance while retaining the anchor algorithm's computational efficiency. From my practitioner perspective, the demonstrated speedups over sampling-based interactive methods and the usability claims (multiword anchors are more natural for users) make this likely to change tooling and workflows for interactive topic modeling. The evaluation is somewhat limited (single dataset, no reported code/hyperparameter details), so adoption will depend on broader empirical validation, but the approach opens compelling, usable directions for building low-latency, human-centered topic tools.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper clearly states its claims and demonstrates empirical benefits of Tandem Anchors versus baselines, but they also concur on important shortcomings: lack of formal guarantees/derivations, limited experimental scope (single dataset), and missing reproducibility details (code, hyperparameters, runtimes). Given the suggestive but not fully rigorous theoretical treatment and the reproducibility/generalization concerns, a cautiously moderate score of 3 reflects that the technical claims are promising but not yet substantiated to a degree warranting higher confidence.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides experiments (on 20NEWS) comparing tandem anchors to single-word anchors and interactive baselines (Fast ITM, Utopian), which partially supports utility and speed claims. However, there are no formal guarantees or analysis of when the pseudoword construction succeeds/fails, mathematical derivations/proofs are not reported as complete, and key reproducibility details (code, hyperparameters, runtime complexity) are missing. The experimental methodology is plausible but limited (single dataset, no mention of strong likelihood-based samplers or statistical tests), and the method relies on assumptions (metadata for seeds, synthetic pseudowords) that are not rigorously justified. Overall the results are suggestive but the paper lacks the theoretical and experimental rigor I would require for higher confidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and supports them with experiments comparing Tandem Anchors to single-word anchors and sampling-based interactive baselines (ITM/Utopian) on 20NEWS using multiple metrics (classification, ARI, VI, coherence, significance), and includes a plausible algorithmic construction (pseudowords and DKL-based convex reconstructions). The mathematical approach (combining facet co-occurrence profiles and solving per-word DKL minimizations) is standard and appears correct, though full proofs or convergence analysis are not emphasized. Experimental methodology is generally sound with appropriate baselines and diverse metrics, but lacks reporting of hyperparameters, code availability, and relies on metadata in some high-water-mark experiments—limitations that introduce some reproducibility and generality concerns. Overall the technical claims are supported, with minor issues around experimental detail and the synthetic nature of pseudowords that do not undermine the main conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work is a practical, non-trivial extension of the anchor-words framework—introducing multiword "tandem anchors" and pseudowords—that meaningfully improves interactivity and scalability compared to single-word anchors and sampling-based ITM. However, it is still an incremental methodological advance rather than a fundamentally new theoretical contribution, lacking formal identifiability guarantees. Given its clear practical utility and methodological novelty, a score of 4 reflects its notable but not revolutionary originality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a natural and practically useful extension of the anchor-words framework—combining anchor facets into pseudowords for interactive topic modeling—which is a clear modification rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The idea is a non-trivial engineering advance (multiword anchors, pseudowords, and user-guided facet construction) and is differentiated from prior single-word anchors and sampling-based ITM baselines, but it lacks formal analysis or identifiability guarantees and reads as an algorithmic heuristic. Given the summary, this is a moderate, incremental novelty: informative to practitioners but unlikely to surprise experts expecting principled extensions of anchor methods.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and practical extension of the anchor-words framework by introducing multiword "tandem anchors" and the pseudoword construction to augment the co-occurrence matrix, which is a non-trivial methodological innovation beyond single-word anchors. It clearly differentiates itself from prior interactive, sampling-based models (e.g., ITM, Utopian) by emphasizing fast, scalable convex reconstructions for interactive use and by showing how human guidance can be injected via multiword facets. The idea is not a wholly new problem, but it supplies a concrete, usable technique that would be informative to experts seeking lower-latency, more interpretable interactive topic methods.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a useful empirical contribution versus single-word anchors and interactive baselines but suffers from limited experimental scope (single dataset), missing ablations/sensitivity analyses, and lack of hyperparameter/runtime/code details that impede reproducibility. Given the empirical support presented is credible but not comprehensive and important analyses are omitted, a middle-ground score is appropriate to reflect moderate substance but clear gaps to address.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is shallow: results are reported only on 20NEWS and comparisons omit stronger likelihood-based baselines (e.g., exact samplers/variational inference), so claims of higher topic quality and interactive usability are under-supported. There are no reported hyperparameters, runtimes, or released code, and no formal analysis or ablation that characterizes when the pseudoword construction succeeds or fails — a serious gap given the method’s heuristic nature. The paper does acknowledge some limitations (synthetic pseudowords, reliance on metadata), but discussion and negative results are not sufficient to offset the reproducibility and theoretical shortcomings.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a solid empirical comparison against single-word anchors and sampling-based interactive baselines on 20NEWS and reports both quality (coherence, clustering metrics) and runtime claims, supporting the main claims. However, it lacks clear ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of facet size or pseudoword construction choices), reports only a single dataset (and relies on metadata in high-water-mark experiments), and omits hyperparameter and code details that hinder reproducibility. The limitations are acknowledged but not fully explored experimentally, so the experimental depth is adequate but missing important analyses for full confidence in practical interactive use.

main_claims: Tandem Anchors (multiword anchors) produce higher-quality topics for interactive topic modeling than single-word anchors.
Tandem Anchors are fast and scalable enough for interactive use, substantially faster than sampling-based interactive topic models (e.g., Interactive Topic Model, Utopian).
Multiword anchors are more intuitive for users and allow straightforward injection of human guidance into the anchor algorithm by constructing pseudowords from anchor facets.
_summary.methodology: Extend the anchor-words algorithm by forming anchor facets (sets of words) and combining their co-occurrence profiles into pseudowords to augment the co-occurrence matrix S; solve per-word convex reconstructions (DKL minimization) against these multiword anchors to obtain coefficients C and recover topic-word distributions A via Bayes’ rule.
baselines: Interactive Topic Model (Hu et al., 2014) / Fast ITM
limitations: Pseudowords constructed from anchor facets do not correspond to real vocabulary words (they are synthetic points).
High-water-mark experiments rely on metadata (titles) to seed tandem anchors; such metadata cannot be assumed available in general.

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in probabilistic modeling and theoretical machine learning with a track record of papers on identifiability and provable algorithms for topic models.
traits: Penalizes papers that introduce algorithmic heuristics (e.g., pseudoword construction) without formal guarantees or analysis of when they succeed/fail
Demands comparisons to the strongest statistical baselines (e.g., exact likelihood-based samplers / variational methods) and downgrades papers that only compare to lightweight or older baselines
Requires rigorous reproducibility: downgrades submissions without published code, detailed hyperparameters, and runtime complexity analyses
Penalizes user-facing claims (e.g., 'more intuitive', 'enables human guidance') that are supported only by informal qualitative examples or small, uncontrolled user tests
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on theoretical soundness and reproducibility; gives high scores only when new methods come with clear formal properties, thorough comparison to strong baselines, and rigorous empirical validation.

reviewer_b.name: Human-in-the-Loop Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Experienced applied NLP researcher specializing in interactive and user-centered machine learning, with several deployed interactive topic modeling tools and user studies in real-world settings.
traits: Rewards papers that demonstrate practical interactivity (low latency, scalability) and real-world usability improvements even if formal proofs are absent
Gives credit for demonstrable human-centered benefits: favors methods with intuitive multiword anchors, qualitative user examples, or small user studies showing improved interpretability

Values engineering clarity and runtime benchmarks: up-scores papers that report wall-clock times, scalability to large vocabularies/corpora, and that compare against sampling-based interactive baselines
Will accept pragmatic algorithmic constructs (e.g., pseudowords, heuristics) if they are shown to be effective, explainable, and useful in practice
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and user-focused: prioritizes practical gains in interactivity, usability, and scalability, and is willing to accept heuristic methods validated by convincing applied experiments.
